issuesFound.title: Missing accessible name on button
issuesFound.explanation: The navigation toggle button has an aria-label but its visible content is a span with aria-hidden="true" containing a hamburger icon (☰). While the aria-label provides an accessible name, the visible text is hidden from screen readers, which is acceptable only if the icon is purely decorative. However, the hamburger icon is functional and conveys meaning (menu toggle), so hiding it with aria-hidden="true" removes a visual cue that sighted users rely on, and the aria-label alone may not sufficiently convey the action's nature without contextual reinforcement. This creates a mismatch between visual and accessible presentation.
issuesFound.fix: Line 28:

issuesFound.title: Missing accessible name on link
issuesFound.explanation: The skip link with href="#main-content" has visible text that is hidden off-screen by default (transform: translateY(-100%)) and only becomes visible on focus. While this is a common pattern for skip links, the accessible name must be perceivable when the link receives focus. However, the issue is not that the name is missing, but that the method of hiding may delay perception. However, per WCAG, skip links are allowed to be hidden until focus, and the accessible name is present in the DOM. The visible text is present in the markup and not hidden from assistive technologies by aria-hidden or similar; it is only visually hidden. Screen readers will read the text when the link receives focus, which is the intended behavior. Therefore, this does not constitute a missing accessible name. The accessible name is present and will be announced when focused. No issue to report.
